title: 👑 Elite challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: the final gate of Module Four

questions.prompt: Complete: "Sarra ___ many kind people during her holiday in Edinburgh." (verb: meet)
questions.explanation: Sarra is one person (she) → "has", and the irregular participle of meet is "met": has met ✓. "Have" is wrong for she, "meet" is the base form, and "meeted" invents a regular ending for an irregular verb.

questions.prompt: Complete: "We have ___ the guidebook about Edinburgh's history." (verb: read)
questions.explanation: The participle of read is spelled the same as the base form — "read" (but pronounced "red"): we have read ✓. "Readed" does not exist, "red" is a colour, and "reads" is the present simple.

options.text: They has visited the old town.
They have visited the old town.
They have visit the old town.
They having visited the old town.

questions.explanation: "They" takes "have", and the regular participle is "visited": They have visited ✓. "Has" is wrong for they, "visit" is the base form, and "having" is the -ing form, which cannot replace the auxiliary here.

questions.prompt: Complete: "We missed the bus — it ___ just ___." (verb: leave)
questions.explanation: The bus (it) takes "has", "just" sits before the participle, and the participle of leave is "left": has just left ✓. "Leaved" invents a regular ending, "have" is wrong for it, and "leave" is the base form.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly talks about a life experience with the present perfect?
questions.explanation: "I" takes "have", "never" goes before the participle, and the participle of be is "been": I have never been abroad ✓. "Went" is the simple past, "being" is the -ing form, and "has" is wrong with I.

questions.prompt: Complete the polite exchange: "'Excuse me, ___ you tell me where the castle is?' 'Yes, ___ straight on.'"
questions.explanation: To ask for information politely we use "Could you tell me…?", and to give the direction we use the base form "go": Could you … go straight on ✓. "Can't" is a negative (wrong here), and "goes/going" are not the right form for a direction.
